title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about food security and the future of farming. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Fields of Change
content.subtitle: An article about global food security and the transformation of agriculture.
content.text:
Feeding a world population that is projected to reach nearly ten billion by 2050 is one of the defining challenges of our time. What makes it especially complex is that the challenge is not simply one of producing more food — in terms of calories, the world currently generates enough to feed everyone. [31] The problem is one of distribution, waste, and the unequal power structures of global food systems, which determine not just who eats, but what they eat, where it comes from, and under what conditions it was grown. [/31]

Agriculture itself sits at a peculiar crossroads. On one hand, it is expected to produce more while consuming fewer resources — less water, less land, fewer chemical inputs. On the other hand, it must adapt to a climate that is already changing the conditions under which crops are grown: longer droughts, more unpredictable rainfall, and rising temperatures in regions that have historically been highly productive. [32] The farmers who bear the greatest burden of these shifts are typically those with the least capacity to adapt — smallholders in the Global South who lack access to insurance, credit, or the technical support that might help them change their practices. [/32]

Technology is frequently presented as the solution to these challenges. Precision agriculture uses sensors, drones, and data analysis to apply water and fertiliser exactly where they are needed, reducing waste and increasing yield. Vertical farming grows crops in stacked indoor layers under artificial lighting, using a fraction of the water required by conventional farming and eliminating the need for pesticides. [33] Proponents of these technologies point to their impressive results in controlled environments. Critics, however, question whether they can be deployed at the scale required to address global food security, and for whom the benefits are likely to be accessible. [/33]

Alternative proteins — foods derived from insects, plants, or laboratory-grown animal cells — have attracted considerable investment and public attention. [34] Their appeal lies not only in their lower environmental footprint compared with conventional livestock farming, but in the argument that they can provide the nutritional profile of meat with a fraction of the land, water, and emissions. [/34] Whether consumers in the world's largest markets will accept these products at scale remains an open question, shaped as much by culture and habit as by nutrition or price.

The debate over the future of farming is often framed as a contest between high-tech industrial solutions and a return to smaller-scale, more ecologically sensitive approaches. In practice, many agricultural scientists argue that the answer lies in a combination of both. [35] Traditional knowledge, developed over generations of observation and accumulated in farming communities around the world, has much to offer alongside new research — not as a romantic alternative to modernity, but as an evidence base that complements it. [/35]

Ultimately, the question of food security cannot be separated from the question of political will. [36] The technologies, the knowledge, and in many cases the resources to ensure adequate nutrition for all currently exist; what is often lacking is the commitment to redistribute them in ways that challenge the interests of established industries and wealthy nations. [/36]

questions.question: The author states that the problem of global hunger is primarily
options: A) a failure to produce sufficient quantities of food worldwide.
B) the result of increasing world population growth.
C) related to inequality and how food systems are organised.
D) caused by a lack of agricultural land in developing regions.
questions.explanation: The author specifies that the issue is 'one of distribution, waste, and the unequal power structures of global food systems', not a shortage of calories.

questions.question: According to the author, the farmers most seriously affected by climate change are those who
options: A) rely on rainfall rather than irrigation for their water supply.
B) grow crops in historically productive regions.
C) have limited means to adjust their farming methods.
D) produce cash crops for export rather than local consumption.
questions.explanation: Those who 'bear the greatest burden' are 'smallholders in the Global South who lack access to insurance, credit, or technical support' needed to adapt.

questions.question: The author's attitude towards technologies like precision agriculture and vertical farming is
options: A) enthusiastic, as they clearly offer the best solution to food insecurity.
B) cautious, as their scalability and accessibility remain uncertain.
C) negative, as they are too expensive for most farmers to use.
D) neutral, as their effectiveness has not yet been tested.
questions.explanation: Critics' questions about 'whether they can be deployed at the scale required' and who the benefits will be 'accessible' to reflect a cautious rather than fully positive stance.

questions.question: The main appeal of alternative proteins, according to the article, is that they
options: A) taste identical to conventional meat products.
B) are already widely accepted by consumers in major markets.
C) can deliver comparable nutrition with a smaller environmental impact.
D) have received more investment than any other food technology.
questions.explanation: Their appeal is described as 'lower environmental footprint' combined with providing 'the nutritional profile of meat with a fraction of the land, water, and emissions'.

questions.question: When the author refers to 'traditional knowledge', the suggestion is that it should be
options: A) preserved as a cultural record even if it is no longer practical.
B) used in combination with modern research, not as a replacement for it.
C) prioritised over high-tech approaches due to its proven effectiveness.
D) shared freely between farming communities to reduce reliance on technology.
questions.explanation: The author describes traditional knowledge as 'an evidence base that complements' new research, 'not as a romantic alternative to modernity'.

questions.question: The author concludes that adequate global nutrition is not achieved mainly because
options: A) the world still lacks the technology to produce enough food.
B) research into food security is underfunded globally.
C) those with power have insufficient motivation to change the current system.
D) climate change has made it impossible to guarantee food production.
questions.explanation: The author states the necessary resources exist but 'what is often lacking is the commitment to redistribute them in ways that challenge the interests of established industries and wealthy nations'.
